disease.name: Stress-Related Mucosal Disease (SRMD)
disease.note: Stress-related mucosal injury in ICU patients leading to upper GI bleeding from gastritis/erosions/ulcers. Major risk factors: mechanical ventilation >48h and coagulopathy (INR >1.5 or platelets <50k). Pathophysiology involves splanchnic hypoperfusion, breakdown of mucosal barrier, and acid back-diffusion. Curling ulcers from burns, Cushing ulcers from TBI. Prophylaxis standard with PPI.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Overt bleeding with instability -> EMERGENCY endoscopy; occult -> optimize prophylaxis

subtypes.name: Superficial Erosive Gastritis (most common)
subtypes.notes: Multiple shallow erosions in gastric fundus/body, usually not bleeding

subtypes.name: Acute Bleeding Ulcer
subtypes.notes: Deeper ulcers with active bleeding, associated with coagulopathy

subtypes.name: Curling Ulcer (burns)
subtypes.notes: Duodenal or gastric ulcers in burn patients >35% BSA

subtypes.name: Cushing Ulcer (TBI)
subtypes.notes: Gastric/duodenal ulcers in TBI, high perforation risk

firstLine.test: NG lavage (if tube in place - coffee ground or bright red)

secondLine.test: BUN/Cr (BUN/Cr ratio >20 suggests upper GI bleed)

investigations.goldStandard: EGD showing gastric erosions/ulcers in fundus/body, sparing antrum in early disease

emergency: IV PPI bolus (80mg pantoprazole) followed by continuous infusion (8mg/h)
EGD for diagnosis and endoscopic therapy (epinephrine injection, cautery, hemoclips)
Angiography with embolization if endoscopic fails
PRBC transfusion for Hb <7-8 g/dL (restrictive strategy in ICU)
inpatient: Stress ulcer prophylaxis for ALL at-risk ICU patients
IV PPI preferred (pantoprazole 40mg daily for prophylaxis, 40mg BID for high risk)
Enteral nutrition as soon as feasible (protective against SRMD)
refractory: Surgical: gastrotomy with under-running of bleeding ulcers (last resort)
preventive: Stress ulcer prophylaxis for MV >48h or coagulopathy
PPI preferred over H2RA for prevention
Enteral nutrition as soon as tolerated

disease.pathophysiology: Critical illness → splanchnic hypoperfusion (shock, vasopressors, reduced cardiac output) → gastric mucosal ischemia → impaired mucus/bicarbonate/prostaglandin production → breakdown of protective mucosal barrier → H+ back-diffusion into mucosa → intracellular acidosis → neutrophil infiltration → inflammatory mediator release → mucosal erosion/ulceration → bleeding. Compounded by coagulopathy from consumption, dilution, and DIC.

complications.complication: Mortality increased in ICU patients who develop overt GI bleeding

mustNotMiss: Mechanical ventilation >48h + coagulopathy are the TWO strongest risk factors - prophylaxis is mandatory
Enteral nutrition is protective against SRMD and should be started early
Overt bleeding in ICU has increased mortality - early EGD is essential
NG tube FALSE NEGATIVE: 15% of UGIB have clear NG aspirate due to intermittent bleeding or pyloric closure

diagnosticClues.clue: ICU patient on mechanical ventilation with coffee-ground NG output + Hb drop
Multiple shallow erosions in gastric fundus/body sparing antrum on EGD
Coagulopathy (INR >1.5, platelets <50k) in ventilated ICU patient
TBI or burn patient with acute GI bleeding

exclusionClues.clue: Known pre-existing PUD with H. pylori (not stress-related)
Cirrhosis with varices (portal hypertension, not stress)
Normal EGD in fundus/body (suggests non-gastric source)
